ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dish — mixed stuffed fried tofu and fish paste items in spiced coconut broth
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Yong Tau Foo; A0FJP (composite dish) is the closest defensible anchor. Exact code unavailable in published FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Yong Tau Foo or equivalent stuffed tofu dish in laksa broth.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker serving of Yong Tau Foo laksa (fried, 7 pieces) is approximately 400 g, comprising 7 assorted deep-fried stuffed items served in a bowl of spiced coconut laksa broth.

nutrient_source.title: Yong tau foo (laksa, 7 pieces, fried) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Yong tau foo (laksa, 7 pieces, fried), CR ID F08010066. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Dishes and Composite Foods — Analogous Estimate Based on Tofu and Fish Paste Stuffed Items in Spiced Broth
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yong Tau Foo (laksa, fried) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy to mixed tofu and fish paste dishes with moderate carbohydrate content (6.36 g/100g), consistent with GI values reported for similar stuffed tofu and mixed vegetable dishes (GI range 40–55) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 25.44) / 100 ≈ 11.4, rounded to 11.6 using 400 g serving size (6.36 g/100g × 4 = 25.44 g carbohydrate per serving). Input record GI confidence was 0.486, reflecting the analogous nature of this estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 495 mg/100g; estimated 1,980 mg per 400 g serving — well above the 600 mg/meal threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to very high sodium from shrimp paste-based laksa broth and seasoned fried pieces; if consumed, request reduced-sodium broth, avoid adding sauces, and restrict portion to no more than 2–3 pieces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6.36 g/100g) and low GI (45) support glycaemic control, but deep-frying increases fat density and caloric load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content and low GI make this dish relatively suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high fat content from deep-frying (8.02 g/100g) increases caloric density, so portion size should be limited to 5–7 pieces and sweet accompaniments such as chili sauce should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (495 mg/100g), elevated saturated fat (~3.2 g/100g) from coconut milk and frying oil, and trans fat (0.1 g/100g) from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish poses a high cardiovascular concern due to the combination of very high sodium, saturated fat from coconut milk-based laksa broth, and pro-inflammatory trans fat generated by deep-frying; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose boiled Yong Tau Foo in clear broth instead and limit sodium intake from all sources on the same day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; sodium exceeds 400 mg/100g RA threshold; nightshade vegetables present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation is a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation; individuals with RA should request boiled pieces in clear broth, avoid nightshade-containing items (eggplant, chili), and note that trans fat at 0.1 g/100g is strongly pro-inflammatory.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yong Tau Foo (Laksa, 7 Pieces, Fried) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish of Hakka Chinese origin, widely adopted across Peranakan and local Chinese food culture. It consists of seven assorted items — typically including tofu, eggplant, bitter gourd, okra, chili, and fish balls — stuffed with a seasoned fish paste mixture and deep-fried before being served in a rich, spiced coconut milk laksa broth flavoured with lemongrass, galangal, shrimp paste (belacan), and dried shrimp. The fried pieces have a crispy exterior with a tender, savoury fish paste interior, and the laksa broth is creamy, aromatic, and moderately spicy. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 116 kcal, 5.02 g protein, 8.02 g fat, and 6.36 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 495 mg primarily from the shrimp paste-based broth and seasoning. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), making the carbohydrate impact relatively modest despite the rich broth. Clinically, the dish is of high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its very high sodium and saturated fat load from coconut milk and deep-frying oil, and is unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation from frying and the presence of nightshade vegetables. It is moderately suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management given its low carbohydrate and low GI profile, provided portion size is controlled and sweet sauces are avoided. The dish is a staple at Singapore hawker centres and food courts, commonly eaten as a lunch or dinner meal.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F08010066) at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, reducing GI sub-confidence to medium, but overall record confidence remains high due to exact HPB source match for all macros and secondary nutrients.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this specific composite Yong Tau Foo laksa (fried, 7 pieces) preparation is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.10 g/100g based on the input health_suitability notes referencing trans fat from deep-frying; this is a plausible estimate for deep-fried items and is consistent with USDA data for similar fried fish paste products. 3. saturated_fat_g of 3.20 g/100g is estimated from the total fat of 8.02 g/100g for a coconut milk and frying oil-based dish, consistent with HPB compositional data for similar laksa-based dishes; exact saturated fat figure not separately reported in the HPB entry for this CR ID. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 5. The input record listed 'vegetarian' as a dietary tag, which is incorrect given the fish paste and shrimp paste content; this has been corrected to is_vegetarian: false. 6. Halal status is marked false as shrimp paste (belacan) and the specific preparation method require verification against MUIS halal certification; consumers should verify with the specific hawker stall.
